segments.text: Right now, as you watch this, a man is sitting in a federal courtroom being sued by the company he helped create,
while Fortune Magazine calls him the world's first potential trillionaire,
while his car company just posted a 71% profit collapse,
and his rocket company is weeks from the biggest IPO in American stock market history.
Same man, same week.
And that somehow is not even the most unbelievable part of his story.
What if I told you this same man once borrowed money for rent, while secretly keeping two
companies from collapse in the same week, that he watched his 10-week-old son die,
that a group of kids beat him so badly as a child he was hospitalized, thrown down a staircase,
kicked until he blacked out. And that from all of that, from a broken kid in South Africa to the
first human being in recorded history to cross $800 billion in personal wealth,
the pattern of how he got there is stranger, darker, and more specific than anything you've
been told. I'm not here to say he's a hero. I'm not here to say he's a villain. Both are too easy.
What I want to show you is one pattern repeating so precisely across 30 years that by the end of this
video you won't be able to unsee it. Pretoria, South Africa, 1971, his mother Mae, Brilliant,
worked multiple jobs, became a sought-after model into her 60s. His father, Errol,
an engineer Elon has publicly called a terrible human being. That is the household we're starting
from. Elon didn't just read, he consumed. Finish the entire Encyclopedia Britannica at nine,
then read it again, taught himself programming at 10. At 12, built a video game called Blastar,
and sold it to a computer magazine for $500. 12 years old. But the mind was never the hard part.
A group of kids beat him so badly he was hospitalized, thrown down a staircase,
kicked until he blacked out. And what he said years later wasn't about the pain.
They got my best friend to lure me out. The betrayal. Remember that. Because the way Elon Musk relates
to trust to the people who get closest to him, it never fully leaves. It shows up in every
boardroom, every marriage, every company. He left South Africa at 17, refusing to serve in a
military enforcing apartheid, America, physics and economics at Penn. Every financial decision from
here forward follows the same irrational, almost deranged logic. 1999. First company Zip 2 sold for
$307 million. Elon's cut $22 million at $27. Most people stopped there. He didn't.
Built X.com merged into PayPal sold to eBay in 2002. His share $175 million. A sensible person
protects it. Rests. Declares victory. Elon took $100 million and launched a private rocket
company. At a time when only governments had ever reached orbit. $70 million into an electric
car startup when the industry's running joke was that EVs were glorified golf carts. Every dollar
gone. One friend told a journalist, I thought he was going to lose everything. And then came the
year that should have ended all of it. But first, the hardest thing. In 2002, the same year he sold
PayPal. Elon and his wife, Justine, lost their first child. A son named Nevada Alexander,
10 weeks old, sudden infant death syndrome. They put him down for a nap. He stopped breathing.
Three days on life support declared brain dead. Justine wrote years later that she was the one
holding him when he died. Elon, she said, made it clear that day he did not want to talk about it.
He buried it. The way he buries everything that threatens to slow him down.
2008. SpaceX had launched the Falcon 1 three times. All three failed. The third carried a memorial
satellite with the cremated remains of over 200 people, including James Duan, the man the world
Scotty from Star Trek. Families had paid for their loved ones to rest among the stars. All of it lost.
SpaceX had money for exactly one more attempt. One. And simultaneously, Tesla was days from bankruptcy.
Elon personally funding payroll from his last reserves. His marriage collapsing, borrowing money for rent.
September 28, 2008. Falcon 1. Fourth and final launch. It worked.
First privately funded liquid fuel rocket to reach orbit in history. Engineers were crying.
Elon, who had held all of it together through sheer will, broke down on camera.
Within months, NASA awarded SpaceX 1.6 billion in contracts. Tesla secured emergency funding on
Christmas Eve, the last possible day before bankruptcy. Both survived by days.
There it is, the pattern. Every time the math says it's over, it isn't.
Hold that thought because it repeats. And the next time the stakes are incomprehensibly larger.
After 2008, the pace became something harder to track. SpaceX landed an orbital rocket booster
back on a ground pad in 2015, first time in history. In 2020, first private company to fly NASA
astronauts to the International Space Station. By 2025, SpaceX was flying more orbital missions than
every other country on Earth, combined. Tesla became the most valuable car company on the planet,
forcing Ford, GM, Toyota and Volkswagen all into electric vehicles. And Starlink,
9,000 active satellites. When Russia's invasion of Ukraine began in 2022,
Minister Mikhailo Fedorov sent one tweet asking Musk for terminals. Musk said yes within 12 hours.
First terminals crossed the Polish border in 48. A soldier identified only as Dima told a journalist,
Starlink is what changed the war in Ukraine's favor. Now they can't blow up our comms.
For soldiers in active combat, losing it didn't mean slower internet. It meant silence. It meant death.
And just when you think you understand the shape of this man's ambition, here is the twist that
reframes everything before it. Elon co-founded OpenAI, funded it with $38 million. Then walked away
in 2018 when he realized he would never control it. Watched Sam Altman turned it into an $850 billion
dollar company. And this week, right now, he is in a federal courtroom in Oakland,
facing Altman directly. On the stand under oath, he told the jury, I came up with the idea,
the name, recruited the key people, taught them everything I know provided all the initial funding.
Those are his exact words. Court transcript, April 28, 2026.
Two days before trial started, he messaged Altman to explore settlement. Altman didn't take it.
Musk's response on record. By the end of this week, you and Sam will be the most hated man in America.
Prediction markets gave him 60% odds of winning the day the trial opened. By the time his own
testimony ended, 34%. His own words hurt his own case. The kid lured out by a friend and beaten at
a staircase is still 40 years later, fighting in courtrooms over betrayal. So what did he build instead?
AI, his own AI company. The Colossus supercomputer in Memphis built in a former appliance factory in
just 122 days, now housing over 200,000 Nvidia GPUs, the largest AI training facility ever built.
And in February, 2026, SpaceX absorbed XAI in the largest private corporate merger ever recorded,
$1.25 trillion. SpaceX has now filed for an IPO targeting $1.75 trillion,
Roadshow June 8, the biggest public offering in American stock market history.
His vision, orbital data centers, AI computing infrastructure built in space itself,
where sunlight never stops. Only Elon Musk solves the AI energy problem by moving the computers off
the planet. And right now in 2026, everything is moving at once. The CyberCab, no steering wheel,
no pedals, incapable of human operation, in continuous production since April. Nurellink's first
patient paralyzed, now plays video games and posts on social media using nothing but thought.
Optimus, Tesla's humanoid robot, already on factory floors, mass production this year.
The same pattern, goals that sound like science fiction, resources entirely real,
timelines that seem impossible, and yet the rockets are flying, the cars changed in industry,
and a paralyzed person is moving a cursor with their mind right now.
But the story has real cracks. Tesla profits collapsed 71% in Q1, 2026. A Yale study found his
Doge political work cost Tesla between 1 and 1.26 million American vehicle sales.
Stocked down 45% from its peak, he himself called Doge a little bit successful.
Not Triumph, regret. Three senior CyberCab leaders departed since production started.
The autonomous software crashes at four times the rate of a human driver,
and X, bought for $44 billion, has blood advertisers, faced legal battles worldwide,
and lost a staggering share of its original value.
Every one of these stumbles looks from a certain angle exactly like 2008 in miniature.
The rational conclusion that it's all unraveling, we've been at that exact conclusion before.
But, and this should genuinely unsettle you, we don't actually know if this time is different,
that's not comforting, it's just honest.
His first wife, Justine, wrote the most accurate sentence ever said about him.
His relentlessness is the most empowering quality imaginable in a CEO,
and the most terrifying quality in a husband.
The same force that launched the rockets, changed the car industry, wired chips into human brains,
and put 9,000 satellites in orbit, burned through marriages, strained relationships with his
children, and left real wreckage running alongside every real achievement.
Maybe the thing that makes someone capable of changing the world is the same thing that makes
them incapable of living inside it normally. Maybe those two things were never separate at all.
This story has no clean ending because it isn't over. The cybercab is on the production line,
the brain chip is scaling. SpaceX's road show begins in June, the largest IPO in American history.
And in a courtroom in Oakland, the man who helped build the AI revolution is fighting, again,
to take something back that he believes was taken from him.
Whatever you think of the man at the center of all of it, you have to admit one thing.
You're still watching. Subscribe because this series is just getting started,
and trust me, the story only gets stranger from here.
